Title: Translator subset reference
Summary: What GDShader features the live preview supports. shader_type canvas_item only; a fixed set of built-ins, hints, uniform types, and operators.
Body:
## shader_type

The live preview supports **`shader_type canvas_item`** only. Any other type (`spatial`, `particles`, `sky`, `fog`) trips the translator with a *"shader_type X isn't in the v1 translator subset"* message. The other types need different vertex stages + different built-in sets; running them on a full-screen quad with the canvas_item built-ins would just produce wrong output, so we fail loudly instead.

You can still **author** shaders of other types in Bleepforge — the editor, save, and usage scanning all work. The live preview just stays in its "unsupported" pane.

## Uniform types

Supported in `uniform <type> name;` declarations:

- `bool` · `int` · `float`
- `vec2` · `vec3` · `vec4`
- `sampler2D` — each user-declared sampler gets its own AssetPicker in the uniform controls and binds to a dedicated WebGL2 texture unit (unit 0 stays reserved for the built-in `TEXTURE` / `u_texture`).

Notably **not** supported:

- `mat2` · `mat3` · `mat4` — 3D-leaning, no v1 use case.
- `usampler*` · `isampler*` · `sampler*Array` · `samplerCube*` — would need GLSL ES 3.00 features we don't expose.

Uniform names that collide with translator-injected symbols (`u_time`, `u_texture`, `u_texture_pixel_size`, `u_resolution`, `v_uv`, `fragColor`, or any local-built-in / constant name) are rejected at parse time — the emitted GLSL would have a duplicate declaration and the WebGL error wouldn't point at the right fix.

## Hints

- `hint_range(min, max[, step])` on `int` or `float` → slider in the UI.
- `source_color` / `hint_color` (aliased) on `vec3` or `vec4` → color picker in the UI.
- `filter_nearest` / `filter_linear` / `filter_nearest_mipmap` / `filter_linear_mipmap` on `sampler2D` → accepted as **no-ops**. The runtime always uses NEAREST + CLAMP_TO_EDGE for pixel-art friendliness. Accepting these hints anyway means shaders copied from Godot tutorials don't need manual editing.
- `repeat_enable` / `repeat_disable` on `sampler2D` → also accepted as no-ops (runtime always CLAMPs).

Unsupported hints (`hint_normal`, `hint_roughness_normal`, `hint_default_white`, `hint_screen_texture`, etc.) are still rejected so a shader that depends on them doesn't silently render wrong.

## Built-ins (locals in main)

These are declared as **local variables at the top of the emitted `main()`** function, so your source references them naturally:

| Name                  | Type   | Source                              |
| --------------------- | ------ | ----------------------------------- |
| `TIME`                | float  | seconds since the preview started   |
| `UV`                  | vec2   | varying from vertex stage (0..1)    |
| `SCREEN_UV`           | vec2   | same as UV for canvas_item          |
| `COLOR`               | vec4   | initialized to `vec4(1.0)`; final value flows back to fragColor |
| `MODULATE`            | vec4   | `vec4(1.0)` (canvas_item context)   |
| `TEXTURE_PIXEL_SIZE`  | vec2   | 1 / texture dimensions              |
| `SCREEN_PIXEL_SIZE`   | vec2   | 1 / canvas dimensions               |

You can read and write `COLOR` — whatever it holds at the end of `main()` becomes the fragment's output. `MODULATE` and the others are read-only conceptually.

## Built-ins (substitutions)

These are **token-rewritten** in your source as it's spliced into the emitted GLSL:

| Name        | Replacement       | Why substitution                  |
| ----------- | ----------------- | --------------------------------- |
| `TEXTURE`   | `u_texture`       | sampler2D can't be a local in GLSL ES 3.00 |
| `FRAGCOORD` | `gl_FragCoord`    | use the native keyword directly   |

## Constants

Emitted as top-level `const float`s in the prelude:

- `PI` = 3.141592653589793
- `TAU` = 6.283185307179586
- `E` = 2.718281828459045

Godot's shader language exposes these; GLSL doesn't, so we declare them.

## Helper functions

Top-level user-defined functions other than `fragment()` ride into the emitted GLSL alongside `main()`. Built-in references in helper bodies — `UV`, `TIME`, `SCREEN_UV`, `MODULATE`, `TEXTURE_PIXEL_SIZE`, `SCREEN_PIXEL_SIZE`, `TEXTURE`, `FRAGCOORD` — are auto-substituted to their underlying uniform / varying / GL keyword so helpers see them naturally without taking long parameter lists. `COLOR` is the one exception: it lives only in `main()`'s scope, so helpers that touch it need to take it as `inout vec4`. See [Helper functions and the main-scope built-ins](/help/shaders/helper-functions) for the full pattern.

`void vertex()` / `void light()` are recognized as entry points but **dropped from the emitted GLSL** — they belong to pipelines the preview can't run.

## What goes through verbatim

Everything inside `void fragment() { ... }` that isn't a built-in-with-substitution gets spliced into the emitted GLSL as-is. That means standard GLSL math (`sin`, `cos`, `mix`, `smoothstep`, `clamp`, `length`, `dot`, …), `texture()` calls, control flow (`if`, `for`, `while`, `return`, `discard`), arithmetic — all work normally.

## What gets rejected at parse time

See [When the live preview says unsupported](/help/shaders/unsupported-pane) for the catalog with workarounds. Short version: `varying`, `hint_screen_texture`, `hint_depth_texture`, `hint_normal_roughness_texture`, `#include`, non-canvas_item `shader_type`, and uniform names that collide with translator-injected symbols.
